opp.ais_why:
• Product scope includes Merlin and Merlin Intake alongside eProcurement and iSaaS. The SF Description emphasizes innovation framing without AI specificity: 'Lapp currently operates an established eProcurement system; however, to meet evolving business needs and ensure long-term scalability, we are seeking a modern, future-proof replacement. The objective of this RFQ is to identify a user-friendly, innovative eProcurement solution capable of supporting our global organization.' Innovation language but not AI-led ‚Äî emphasis is on usability and phased rollout.
• Formal Evaluation stage with 1 Avoma meeting on 16 Apr 2026 (~45 min) with Eleni Jakovidou and Iris Lechner from Lapp. BD_Engagement is blank. Last activity 11-Jun-2026 (a future scheduled date). Description further states: 'The new platform should enable streamlined procurement processes, intuitive usability, and the flexibility to be rolled out in phased deployments across multiple regions.' Phased multi-region rollout is operational scope, not AI scope.
• Medium confidence: Merlin scoped but the Description and the only available Avoma meeting are replacement/usability-driven with no AI demand signals. Held at 4. Would move UP if a deeper demo surfaces Lapp asking about Merlin agents or autonomous workflows; would drop to 3 if the engagement remains replacement-focused only.

metrics.narrative: No quantified business case or ROI documented across Next Step history, contact-role fields, or available evidence. Next Step 20 Apr notes 'Market evaluation, currently using GEP (OpusCapita)' which signals an incumbent replacement motion, but the buyer has not articulated cost savings, cycle-time reduction, error-rate improvement, or headcount impact. The April 16 call (per Next Step) and June 11 workshop are described generically ('more info to be discovered') with no capture of pain quantification. Lapp is running a formal RFP (Next Step 7 Apr 'RFP running', Next Step 27 Mar 'RFP received') which typically includes a business case, but we have not seen it. Without quantified metrics the deal cannot progress to a defensible ROI workshop or EB-level value presentation. First action: debrief today's June 29 demo to extract the business problem and any quantified pain the buyer stated, then build the value case and validate it with the Economic Buyer once identified.

champion.narrative: No strong champion identified. Eleni Jakovidou (Buyer) and Iris Lechner (Senior buyer) are the primary contacts across April and June, but their behavior shows interest, not advocacy. They attended the April 16 call and June 11 workshop and are scheduled for today's June 29 demo, which demonstrates ongoing engagement. However, there is no evidence of either introducing Zycus to the Economic Buyer, coaching on the internal process, sharing the RFP scorecard, advocating for Zycus over GEP/iValua, or driving urgency. Eleni explicitly demanded Copilot-level AI on April 16 (per known requirements), which Zycus can satisfy with Merlin, but there is no evidence she has championed that differentiation internally or pushed back on competitors who lack it. Mike Wagner (Senior SAP-Berater) is a contact role but has not appeared in Next Step or task history, so his engagement level and advocacy are unknown. No coach or internal sponsor has been identified. The SRM decision owner Thomas Wenger has not been engaged by Zycus despite owning the decision that may kill this deal. Champion status is 'developing' at best: Eleni and Iris are engaged and responsive (scheduling demos, participating in workshops) but are not driving the deal forward or providing insider access. Immediate action: after today's demo, ask Eleni or Iris to introduce Zycus to the Economic Buyer and Thomas Wenger, share the evaluation scorecard and internal timeline, and explicitly ask one of them to advocate for Zycus in the shortlisting decision. If neither will advocate, identify who will.

competition.narrative: Four confirmed competitors in a formal RFP process: GEP (OpusCapita) is the incumbent (Next Step 20 Apr 'currently using GEP (OpusCapita)'); iValua won the prior SRM competition and is the primary threat (Next Step 14 Apr 'Lost SRM with them, understanding competion. iValua win so question on whether to stay in or opt out'); Evalua and Mercanis are also in the evaluation (inferred from prior sweep competitive landscape, not explicitly named in current Next Step but carried forward as known competitors). The SRM/eProcurement consolidation risk makes iValua the most dangerous: if Thomas Wenger selects iValua for SRM (or Evalua), Lapp may consolidate eProcurement under that vendor to simplify integration and vendor management, killing the Zycus opportunity even if Zycus scores well on eProcurement functionality. Next Step 20 Apr 'Decision regarding SRM not final yet' confirms the SRM decision is still open, which means the consolidation risk is live. GEP is the defensive incumbent but is vulnerable (Lapp is running an RFP to replace them); Zycus's wedge is AI/Merlin (Eleni's explicit demand for Copilot-level AI, which GEP and iValua likely do not offer at that level). However, the wedge has not been activated: no evidence Merlin was demonstrated at the June 11 workshop, and today's June 29 demo is the next opportunity. If Zycus does not land the Merlin narrative and does not engage Thomas Wenger to influence the SRM decision, iValua will consolidate both SRM and eProcurement. How we win: (1) demonstrate Merlin's Copilot-level AI on today's demo and make it the centerpiece of the RFP scorecard differentiation; (2) engage Thomas Wenger immediately to understand SRM timing, position Zycus as the unified SRM+eProcurement platform (if applicable) or the superior eProcurement integration partner for iValua/Evalua (if SRM is separate); (3) build a champion (Eleni or Iris) who will advocate for Zycus internally and coach on the SRM/eProcurement decision interdependency.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is inferred from the RFP motion and incumbent replacement context but not explicitly captured. Next Step 20 Apr states 'Market evaluation, currently using GEP (OpusCapita)', which signals dissatisfaction with the incumbent sufficient to run a formal multi-vendor RFP. The pain owner is likely VP Procurement or a senior supply-chain executive (not yet identified), with operational execution owned by Eleni Jakovidou (Buyer) and Iris Lechner (Senior buyer). The specific operational or financial pain driving the replacement (e.g. GEP's user experience, integration gaps, cost, lack of AI/automation, supplier network limitations) has not been articulated by the buyer on record. The one explicit pain point documented is Eleni's dissatisfaction with AI capabilities in the evaluated systems (Copilot-level AI demand from April 16 call context, carried forward as a known requirement). No other pain quantified or quoted. The April 16 call and June 11 workshop were opportunities to capture pain in the buyer's words, but Next Step only records logistics ('Call with Lapp on 16.4.', 'Demo on 11/6 - more info to be discovered') without pain capture. Without a clear pain narrative Zycus cannot anchor the value proposition, differentiate against GEP/iValua, or build a compelling business case for the Economic Buyer. Immediate action: debrief today's June 29 demo to extract the pain Eleni and Iris articulated, quote their language, tie it to Merlin and the Zycus platform, and formalize it into the ROI narrative.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified or engaged after 90 days. The three contact roles are all operational: Mike Wagner (Senior SAP-Berater), Eleni Jakovidou (Buyer), Iris Lechner (Senior buyer). None control budget or contract authority for a 115K USD SaaS deal. Next Step history references Thomas Wenger as the SRM decision owner (Apr 14 'Lost SRM with them, understanding competion. iValua win so question on whether to stay in or opt out', Apr 20 'Decision regarding SRM not final yet') but Thomas is not in the contact-role list and has not been engaged by Zycus, so his budget authority and relationship to eProcurement procurement are unknown. No CFO, VP Procurement, or C-level sponsor appears in any field, task, or Next Step. The April 16 call and June 11 workshop were conducted with operational buyers only. A 115K contract (plus 195K implementation per the May 8 quote) requires executive sign-off; without EB access Zycus cannot negotiate pricing, align on commercials, or finalize contract. The SRM/eProcurement vendor consolidation risk (if Thomas picks iValua or Evalua for SRM, procurement may consolidate under that platform) means the EB may be the same executive who owns the SRM decision or their peer. Immediate action: map the approval chain, identify who controls the eProcurement and SRM budgets, and secure an executive meeting within 14 days.

decision_process.narrative: Lapp is running a multi-vendor RFP process: RFP received 27 Mar, clarification questions submitted 10 Apr, RFP response submitted by 24 Apr (Next Step history). Competing vendors are GEP (OpusCapita, the incumbent per Next Step 20 Apr), iValua (who won the prior SRM competition per Next Step 14 Apr), Evalua, and Mercanis (inferred from competitive landscape). The process includes RFI/RFP rounds followed by vendor demos (June 11 workshop completed, June 29 demo scheduled today). However, the downstream process is unclear: no shortlisting timeline, no commercials or ROI workshop date, no EB presentation scheduled, no contract kickoff milestone. The call sequence shows operational buyers only (Eleni, Iris, Mike) across April and June; no executive has joined, which is unusual for a deal 90 days into evaluation and 185 days from close. The SRM decision process is separate and blocking: Thomas Wenger owns SRM vendor selection, iValua won the prior SRM round, and the decision is 'not final yet' (Next Step 20 Apr). If Thomas selects iValua or Evalua for SRM, Lapp may consolidate eProcurement under that vendor, killing the Zycus opportunity (Next Step 14 Apr 'iValua win so question on whether to stay in or opt out'). The approval chain is unknown: who signs contracts, whether SRM and eProcurement budgets are separate or unified, whether Thomas or a CFO/VP Procurement is the final approver. Immediate action: after today's demo, ask Eleni/Iris for the evaluation timeline (shortlist date, finalist decision, contract signature target), map the approval chain, and engage Thomas Wenger to understand SRM timing and integration vs consolidation intent.

The objective of this RFQ is to identify a user-friendly, innovative eProcurement solution capable of supporting our global organization.' Innovation language but not AI-led ‚Äî emphasis is on usability and phased rollout.
• Formal Evaluation stage with 1 Avoma meeting on 16 Apr 2026 (~45 min) with Eleni Jakovidou and Iris Lechner from Lapp. BD_Engagement is blank. Last activity 11-Jun-2026 (a future scheduled date). Description further states: 'The new platform should enable streamlined procurement processes, intuitive usability, and the flexibility to be rolled out in phased deployments across multiple regions.' Phased multi-region rollout is operational scope, not AI scope.
• Medium confidence: Merlin scoped but the Description and the only available Avoma meeting are replacement/usability-driven with no AI demand signals. Held at 4. Would move UP if a deeper demo surfaces Lapp asking about Merlin agents or autonomous workflows; would drop to 3 if the engagement remains replacement-focused only.
